MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CUD-22-669088 WITH CASE CPF-21-517468
Off Calendar Aug-05-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

RESPONDENT STEVEN HO'S MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CUD-22-669088 WITH CASE CPF-21-517468.

Defendant Ho's motion to consolidate cases CUD-22-669088 and CPF-21-517468 is denied. The cases do not share common issues of law and fact. The civil action relates to personal loans or money allegedly due for personal services between plaintiff Leung and defendant Ho. The unlawful detainer is brought by a third-party landlord against Ho and Paramount for possession when Ho exclusively controlled the restaurant. Plaintiff is not part of the unlawful detainer. Martin-Bragg v. Moore (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 367 is inapposite since complex title issues do not need to be resolved to determine the unlawful detainer case. In fact, plaintiff Leung admits that she has no interest in the tenant restaurant Paramount. (See Amended Complaint, par. 39.) The court notes that the trial date for the unlawful detainer is August 26, 2022, and the civil action currently has no trial date. Consolidation will unduly prejudice the landlord. Consolidation will lead to delay, more unlawful detainer damages, and thwart the summary nature of the unlawful detainer proceeding.

Order is signed.

Judge: Vedica Puri, Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/EPS)

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT STEVEN HO'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT AGAINST STEVEN HO.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Pro per defendant Stephen Ho's motion to set aside default is granted. It is a policy of California's courts to decide cases on their merits. Ho filed his motion to set aside less than three months after default was entered. (See CCP 473.) The declarations submitted establish "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." (Id.) No attorney fees are awarded. I find no default judgment in the court's register of actions for this case.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the Court's ruling.
